review_text: Returning to Database Systems as a required track course, I kept up with nearly every lecture but still felt rusty, and I barely squeaked by this term. The teacher can explain concepts clearly, but the real examples were thin, so I spent extra time trying to map ideas to something concrete I could actually code. I appreciated that the prerequisite gaps were filled better than I expected, and the fast feedback on quizzes kept me from spiraling, even though the explanations behind the marks felt lightweight. Time pressure was usually manageable, and I did notice the work was mostly individual, which didn’t help when I wanted a quick sanity check with a classmate. I remember one office-hours chat where we walked through a tricky join, and that small moment kept me going, but I still can’t honestly recommend it despite some value.

I took Machine Learning with Prof. Cohen mainly because I needed it for a specialization, and I wasn’t sure what I’d signed up for—proofs make sense to me, but systems work tends to stress me out, so I came in feeling cautiously confident yet wary of the tooling maze. The pacing dragged in the middle of the term, especially when deadlines kept piling up and the tooling hiccups kept throwing me off; the platform would glitch or the repo wouldn’t sync right when I was juggling another hard class, and it felt like I was scrambling to regather momentum. I did feel like I learned a lot, especially by the late-semester turning point when we finally got a clearer sense of the core ideas, but the overall experience left me with a constant sense of drift—the lectures are recorded and useful, but they read as dry and rarely sparked much engagement, which didn’t help when attendance faded. The requirements skew toward report-heavy work and lengthy writeups, which is fine for me since I can follow proofs, but the feedback came sparsely until it was basically too late to fix anything meaningful. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared to push through the rough patches with solid time management and a tolerance for late feedback.

The Database Systems course in my last semester felt a bit rough around the edges; the grading rubric was vague and the prerequisite feel like more than advertised, but I still kept up with a solid B. The midterm crunch didn’t help, and email support was slow, though feedback came quickly enough to revise.

I took Cyber Security Basics mostly because it’s needed for a specialization, and I’m comfortable with proofs but not so much with systems work; the lectures were clear and paced fine, but the platform kept failing before deadlines and the feedback dragged in too late, which really stung during the graded assignment 3, where I struggled with the practical questions.

Taking Machine Learning in my first semester while juggling a full-time job felt like a sprint with a few long uphill stretches. Dr. Miller’s lectures were solid when I could attend, but the ramp-up quickly got dense, and the prerequisites started accessible and then slammed into real data work, which made catching up on evenings I had left besides work feel impossible at times. The forums helped, yet the responses sometimes sounded dismissive, like my basic questions were treated as off-topic noise, which wore on me. The assessment pattern leaned on heavy reports and time-consuming writeups, more than I expected, and the grading felt opaque—I’d hand in something I thought was clear and be unsure which parts blew the grade. I used the materials selectively—watching a few lectures here and there when a deadline loomed—so I didn’t build a steady study habit, and I ended up relying on one or two project elements rather than the full set of practicals. Compared to another course from the term, this one demanded more solo slog and had me thinking twice about taking it again, even though some code and a tool I learned did stick with me.

So, I’m in the middle of a heavy semester, trying to fit this ML course into short evening study sessions, and I barely passed after I wrestled with it most of the term. The lectures are useful once you’re able to sit through them, but they’re mostly dry and hard to stay engaged with after a long day. I liked that the materials were solid and the core ideas were laid out clearly enough to follow along if I forced myself to take notes, but the whole tool setup took more time than the payoff, which was frustrating when you’re juggling other classes. Most of the work was solo, with little peer dependency, so I spent more time chasing answers in forums than actually collaborating, which felt oddly isolating. The staff weren’t as responsive as I’d hoped, and it seemed like students helped each other more than the instructors or TAs did, which made the support feel adrift. A concrete moment that sticks: I spent an entire evening wrestling with configuring the experiment runner, only to realize the grading quirks skewed the deadline by a day, which added unnecessary stress. I’d probably avoid taking it again, even though it did push me to figure things out, but I’m glad I learned something workable from the grind.

I took Machine Learning as a required track course, and I stuck with nearly every lecture even though I’m coming from a strong coding background but weak on the theory side. The instructor has a knack for big-picture explanations, but the actual examples felt thin and sometimes felt like they were skimming over the hard parts I was hoping to see demonstrated. The tooling setup, on the other hand, was rough enough to slow me down more than once, and the project-heavy grading with high-stakes exams was a stress pattern I didn’t fully adapt to, especially since the prerequisites feel like they’re best if you already know adjacent material. Still, it did push me to connect coding to models more concretely, and I barely passed after wrestling through most of the term, which has me mildly glad I didn’t bail earlier.

Cyber Security Basics with Dr. Park was clear at first and paced okay, but as mid-semester hit I started slipping behind since attendance faded while juggling a full-time work schedule. The heavy report load felt fair yet exhausting, and collaboration expectations were unclear, which didn’t help someone new to the discipline. I kept thinking it would click, but the prerequisite feel and the time sink kept dragging me down.

I’m new to this stuff, and Cyber Security Basics started out rough because I kept tripping over a confusing early concept, which made the rest feel exhausting even though the lectures were clear and paced fine. The grading rubrics were vague, but I’d still recommend it to others starting out.

This Calculus II elective felt like wandering through a math maze while my brain kept forgetting how to thread the basics, and yeah, the difficulty was a constant pebble in my backpack. I’m a returning student trying to rust off the math from a few years ago, so the lectures are useful enough to jog memory, but they’re pretty dry and sometimes drag, especially when the recordings skip through steps I want to rewatch. The forums help me feel less alone, but I’ve seen a few threads that read dismissive or like people are racing to be the smartest, which didn’t help my rusty confidence. Most weeks I was juggling short evening study sessions, and the time pressure was manageable, but feedback rolled in sparse until right before deadlines—which felt like a last-minute nudge rather than steady guidance. The independent work mostly suited me, since I didn’t need a lot of peer pull, though I wish the structure encouraged a bit more collaboration. One memorable moment was that brutal week around the midterm where I realized I’d forgotten a common substitution trick, and I scrambled to patch it up last minute. Overall, I’m sticking with it, but I’d hoped for quicker notes on mistakes.

I started Cyber Security Basics hopeful, but the grading left me frustrated and I felt review quality shift a lot by assignment. I’m new to this stuff, and the lectures were useful though dry, while midterm crunches and mostly solo work didn’t help the vibe.

I started Cyber Security Basics hopeful, but the grading left me frustrated and I felt review quality shift a lot by assignment. I’m new to this stuff, and the lectures were useful though dry, while midterm crunches and mostly solo work didn’t help the vibe.

I signed up for Operating Systems mainly because I’m curious how stuff like schedulers and memory management actually work, but the course structure made it feel like you had to hunt for the signal through a lot of noise. The lectures felt practical in a way that kept things organized—slides were serviceable and I appreciated that the core ideas showed up in the right places, even if they didn’t stick in my memory the way a catchy diagram would. My background is strong in coding but weak on theory, so I mostly used the materials when I hit a roadblock or needed a quick reference rather than following every week’s cadence. The setup for the tooling took more effort than I expected, and I ended up spending extra time just getting the environment to run, which didn’t always pay off in deeper understanding. I relied on forums, office hours, and the TAs to parse deadlines and ambiguous instructions, since the admin side left too much room for interpretation and I didn’t want to chase minor ambiguities. By the end I barely passed after struggling most of the term, and I wouldn’t say the delivery left a lasting impression, but I learned a lot in a messy, hard-won way.

I took Cyber Security Basics mostly because it’s needed for a specialization, and I’m comfortable with proofs but not so much with systems work; the lectures were clear and paced fine, but the platform kept failing before deadlines and the feedback dragged in too late, which really stung during the graded assignment 3, where I struggled with the practical questions.

Data Structures was an advanced elective I needed for the specialization, and I still think I learned solid stuff, even though the tooling setup was a pain and the materials felt thin at times. The lectures were clear and the pacing reasonable, but I fell behind around deadlines and only caught up during crunch weeks; office hours saved me more than once, especially when a quick debugging session revealed where I was going wrong on the project.

Dr. Miller was clear most of the time, and I kept up with nearly every lecture in Computer Networks, but the grading comments came too late to help, and tooling issues during the week of the big exam lingered; one memorable week was that tricky TCP practice project.

I’m in my first semester and took Linear Algebra with Prof. Cohen while juggling a full-time job, so accessibility in catching up has been a big plus—the lectures break things down conceptually, even if the examples feel a bit thin at times. I appreciated quick feedback on assignments that let me adjust my approach, but the delays on some feedback items meant I had to keep a mental note and move on anyway. The forums and office hours help a lot, though a few threads were recently dismissive when I asked for clarification on a matrix decomposition step I didn’t fully grasp yet. I’d only recommend the course if you’re prepared to put in extra time and skim through the forums for quick nudges before the next deadline.

Starting Cyber Security Basics in my first semester felt like jumping into a puzzle where I could see the pieces but not the full picture, and my strong coding background helped me survive, though the theory parts left me scratching my head. I remember a moment early on when I misread a lab prompt and spent hours chasing a wrong assumption, which shaped my approach for most of the term and made me more anxious about every subsequent assignment until the rubric finally clarified itself—sort of. The mix of assessments never quite matched the lectures for me: we had tight deadlines for weekly quizzes and a big practical project that demanded more design thinking than code, and the grading rubric still felt vague, with comments arriving late enough that I’d already moved on to the next topic. Still, the course did fill some gaps I had, especially around getting a realistic sense of what a security review looks like, and the hands-on parts with the small incident-response drill helped me connect the dots in a way the lectures often didn’t. The instructor was approachable, which I appreciated, even if the overall grading felt like a friction point I had to navigate more than learn from, and I ended up barely passing after a rough term.

Database Systems was a slog more than I expected, but the pacing finally clicked once I got past that early bug in the query optimizer and stopped fighting the theory gaps I had. The term felt packed with deadlines, yet the work was doable since the TAs answered fast and gave practical fixes; I barely squeezed a pass, but I saw how it builds on the prerequisites and the projects weren’t random fluff.

Taking Computer Networks this term basically became my main focus, and I leaned on recordings and notes a lot because the live lectures didn’t stick for me, even though the slides were serviceable. I appreciated the practical bite in the project where we mapped a small network and performance measurements, but the grading comments dragged a bit after the deadline, which left the mid-semester crunch feeling even rougher.

I’m in my first semester and took Linear Algebra with Prof. Cohen while juggling a full-time job, so accessibility in catching up has been a big plus—the lectures break things down conceptually, even if the examples feel a bit thin at times. I appreciated quick feedback on assignments that let me adjust my approach, but the delays on some feedback items meant I had to keep a mental note and move on anyway. I’d only recommend the course if you’re prepared to put in extra time and skim through the forums for quick nudges before the next deadline.

Midway through the semester in Machine Learning, I’m feeling mixed but mostly stuck in the middle of a heavy crunch. The quick feedback on scores comes through fast, but the explanations feel pretty thin, which makes it hard to tell if a C actually means I’m missing the point or just not getting the phrasing right yet. I’m new to the discipline, so I’m still learning the basics, and the few assessments that actually matter—there aren’t many, but they loom large—have me scrambling to catch up around deadlines. I do better when I have a study group, and this course kind of shines there; chatting through a confusing concept helps more than the lecture slides, which feel a bit boilerplate. The tooling setup adds friction—setting up the environment and chasing library versions ate into learning time, and I’m not sure the payoff matches the effort. Compared to another course I took this term, this one dragged a bit during the mid–semester crunch, even though the exam felt fair and the material aligned with what I’m trying to grasp, if not mastered yet. Overall, I’m still figuring out whether I’m getting the right signal from my work.

I’m comfortable with proofs, but Database Systems pushed me in a direction I didn’t expect, and I felt the material was solidly organized, especially the conceptual explanations Dr. Singh gave. The examples felt a bit thin, which didn’t help my recall when I got stuck on early concepts, and I fell behind for a stretch before catching up near deadlines. TA support was genuinely practical and quick, which helped, though feedback sometimes arrived too late to adjust my approach in time. I’d recommend this course only if you’re ready to build a study group and come in with some momentum, because the workload is neutral and the real value comes from those focused discussions and the way the group pieces things together.

Honestly, I’m new to the discipline and the basics were already a lot to juggle, but this course felt like the required checkpoint I had to clear for the track. I kept up with nearly every lecture, and peer discussions sometimes helped more than the official materials, which was both reassuring and a little frustrating when the answers came too late or didn’t fit my questions. The toolset started rough for me—yes, the software stack was clunky at first—but I managed to get through it, and the later work felt more doable, if not elegant. There was a late-semester turning point that finally clicked, and I realized the prerequisites fit me better than I expected, even as I doubted the pacing and the sparse feedback until it was nearly over.

Machine Learning with Dr. Chen was tougher than I expected for an advanced elective, and keeping up while juggling other hard courses was brutal. The setup for tools felt like a time sink, but lectures were clear and deadlines piled up.

Taking Computer Networks in my last semester, I enjoyed the challenge enough to push through, even though the weekly feel was uneven and some instructions left a lot open to interpretation. The few big assessments mattered a lot, and with a strong coding background I could code around the concepts, but the forums could be helpful yet sometimes felt dismissive. Not sure I’d fully recommend it, but the curiosity kept me going.

Data Structures was an advanced elective I needed for the specialization, and I still think I learned solid stuff, even though the tooling setup was a pain and the materials felt thin at times. The lectures were clear and the pacing reasonable, but I fell behind around deadlines and only caught up during crunch weeks; office hours saved me more than once, especially when a quick debugging session revealed where I was going wrong on the project.

I’m in Computer Networks this semester, juggling it with a couple of other heavy courses, and I’ve found it mostly doable but exhausting in some parts. The class feels like an introductory service course that assumes you’ll pick up concepts quickly, and I remember getting stuck on the early idea of routing tables—once that clicked later, the rest of the term sort of followed, but that initial confusion echoed through many weeks. Dr. Miller lectures are clear enough, but the way updates and policy notes are communicated feels sloppy at times, so I relied on recordings and my own notes more than anything the staff actually circulating. The platform setup took more time than the payoff, and the tooling headaches rarely lined up with the actual assignments, which was frustrating during a midterm crunch. I’ve noticed the course works best if you team up with a study group, since the collaboration structure makes more sense when you bounce ideas off others, and the peer help often outpaced staff responses. By the end I’m not sure I’d recommend it outright, but I’ll admit the hands-on parts grew on me, even if the design of assessments left me tired and a bit unsure about where I stood.

This summer OS course felt like sprinting through a cloud of jargon while juggling a full-time job, and I mostly lived on recordings and notes. The grading rubric was vague, and the prerequisite vibe was tougher than advertised, so I felt a bit set up to fail. Office hours rescued me once I asked about a flaky process for the project, but collaboration expectations remained murky. It’s learned a lot, but I still disliked the ride.

Cyber Security Basics with Dr. Park was clear at first and paced okay, but as mid-semester hit I started slipping behind since attendance faded while juggling a full-time work schedule. The heavy report load felt fair yet exhausting, and collaboration expectations were unclear, which didn’t help someone new to the discipline. I kept thinking it would click, but the prerequisite feel and the time sink kept dragging me down.

Taking Cyber Security Basics in the middle of a brutal semester, I stuck around mostly because the schedule fit better than my real interest in the topics, and yes, I’m comfortable with proofs but not really the systems-heavy parts, so I felt a bit out of my element more often than I’d like. The design of assessments rubbed me the wrong way: the quizzes felt rushed and the problem sets seemed to hinge on a single tricky twist rather than a clear through-line, which left me frustrated but still slogging through to the return date. Tooling setup was a pain—getting the environment running on my laptop took longer than any actual lab work, and I spent more hours chasing dependencies than learning anything practical. The slides are serviceable but not memorable, and I can’t shake the feeling that I walked away with ideas rather than concrete takeaways, even though the prerequisite material built on what was stated. Office hours rescued the course for me more than once, a small mercy on weeks with inconsistent motivation and time pressure that was manageable most weeks. One week stands out: a surprisingly tough exam week where I memorably blanked on a specific networking diagram, and that moment echoed how the design didn’t align with how I study best. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared for rough tooling days and you know you’ll actually show up for the support and push through the pace.

Linear Algebra was this oddly uneven ride mid-semester, especially since I started the term hopeful about an advanced elective and ended barely passing after struggling most of the term. The course felt disorganized at times—deadlines shifted, and the weekly problem sets piled up with little clear guidance on what exactly the grader was looking for, which made the grading feel opaque. I liked the instructor when they explained concepts in class, but the feedback often arrived too late to help before the next deadline, and the detailed comments came long after I’d already moved on. I used the materials selectively, mostly revisiting the notes and a couple of practice exams, and I’ll admit the ramp-up felt sharper than I expected for something billed as accessible at first. The project on eigenvectors was the most concrete anchor, though the rest blurred into a mix of quick lectures and email replies that didn’t quite hit the mark.

Early on I got tangled around the idea of Big-O as a rulebook, and that confusion stuck, shaping how I approached the first problem set and the midterm that followed—plus the group chat where we mostly end up reminding each other of the due dates rather than getting clear staff guidance. The support from peers saved me more than once, even as the instructions left a lot to interpretation.

Calculus II felt like it fit my schedule more than my curiosity, and starting out I was basically new to the discipline, learning the basics while balancing a couple of other demanding courses. The materials were not the breeze I hoped for—I used the handouts and notes only when I needed a quick refresher, since some sections felt buried in crowded PDFs and a few practice sets were oddly misaligned with what we covered in lecture. I found the instruction uneven week to week, with some days you could actually follow the logic and others where I left more confused than when I walked in, which didn’t help since the prerequisite starts accessible but then ramps up sharply. Deadlines always felt rough during the week with tool glitches—our online grader would hiccup just as I was compiling a multi-part assignment, and that stress spilled into the next day’s study block. Office hours rescued the course for me, though: I’d swing by with a concrete question about a specific integration by parts setup, and a TA would walk through the exact step I’d bungled, which made the difference. Compared to another course I took that term, this one was messier in pacing but the forum threads and a couple of TA-led clarifications kept me from dropping out entirely.

I signed up for Database Systems out of curiosity, and the first week left me a bit tangled because I came in thinking I’d need less boilerplate than usual, but the lectures were clearer than I expected and moved at a decent pace. The summer term pacing felt compressed, and I had enough time to keep up, though motivation drifted in and out; the workload was heavy on reports and the feedback came fast, but sometimes the explanations felt thin. Still, I’d recommend it to others who already know a bit of adjacent material and want a solid, practical push.

I’m in my last semester and this advanced elective still felt like it dragged more than it should have, mostly because the workload was heavy and the tooling kept misfiring right when I needed to meet a deadline. Prof. Cohen is solid enough in person, but the course relies so much on long, report-heavy assignments that I spent more time formatting and chasing data than actually thinking through the concepts, which was rough given I had enough time but inconsistent motivation. The forums helped sometimes, though they could be dismissive when questions touched on the quirks of the workflow, and I found the peer discussions to be where I learned the most, especially when comparing notes on how others approached the same data model exercises. The real kicker was the deadline pattern—stacked weeks with multiple deliverables and a few tool hiccups that made those weeks feel endless, like the deadline weeks were intentionally harsher. Compared to another course in the term, this one demanded more time spent on compiling a coherent report than on understanding the core ideas, but I would still recommend it to people who want the privilege of wresting with real, heavy data setups. One standout moment was a late-evening incident where the database export failed and we had to improvise a backup plan; it stuck with me because it showed the fragility of the tooling in practice.

Midway through the heavy stretch of this semester, I’m grappling with whether the material really clicked, but I’ll admit the peer discussion has saved me more than the official readings. Dr. Singh delivers clear lectures with solid pacing, and the way we bounce ideas in small groups often clarifies things the slides gloss over, which helps my understanding even when I’m unsure about the math-heavy parts. The course fills in prerequisite gaps better than I expected, and I ended up relying on quick forum threads and the TAs during office hours more than the staff did, especially when a concept like process scheduling didn’t click right away. I’d say collaboration structure matters here; chatting with a partner helped more than slogging through the forum posts alone. I’m on the fence about the final grade; a C feels like it could reflect my grasp of the core ideas, not just the messy week-to-week work, and I’m still unsure if I truly understand some of the depth. There’s a real upside, though: practical application shows up in how we discuss real OS quirks during peer discussions, which I actually found useful for applying concepts to projects.

I’m glad I stuck with Data Structures, even though the setup felt a little heavier than advertised and I’m not really a systems person. Prof. Rios is solid, but the course assumes more prior knowledge than I expected, so I spent early evenings scrambling through proofs and data-wrangling basics to keep up. The stack we used started rough—a few quirks in the tooling—but once I found a study group, things clicked and the projects actually made sense, especially the late-semester turning point where a messy last-minute bug finally clicked with the algorithm idea. The workload is heavy on reports, and it wears you down even when the core ideas are fair; still, the sense of progress when a tough implementation finally works is real.

I took Machine Learning with Prof. Cohen mainly because I needed it for a specialization, and I wasn’t sure what I’d signed up for—proofs make sense to me, but systems work tends to stress me out, so I came in feeling cautiously confident yet wary of the tooling maze. The pacing dragged in the middle of the term, especially when deadlines kept piling up and the tooling hiccups kept throwing me off; the platform would glitch or the repo wouldn’t sync right when I was juggling another hard class, and it felt like I was scrambling to regather momentum. I did feel like I learned a lot, especially by the late-semester turning point when we finally got a clearer sense of the core ideas, but the overall experience left me with a constant sense of drift—the lectures are recorded and useful, but they read as dry and rarely sparked much engagement, which didn’t help when attendance faded. The requirements skew toward report-heavy work and lengthy writeups, which is fine for me since I can follow proofs, but the feedback came sparsely until it was basically too late to fix anything meaningful. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared to push through the rough patches with solid time management and a tolerance for late feedback.

I started Cyber Security Basics hopeful, but the grading left me frustrated and I felt review quality shift a lot by assignment. I’m new to this stuff, and the lectures were useful though dry, while midterm crunches and mostly solo work didn’t help the vibe.

Starting Cyber Security Basics in my first semester felt like jumping into a puzzle where I could see the pieces but not the full picture, and my strong coding background helped me survive, though the theory parts left me scratching my head. I remember a moment early on when I misread a lab prompt and spent hours chasing a wrong assumption, which shaped my approach for most of the term and made me more anxious about every subsequent assignment until the rubric finally clarified itself—sort of. The mix of assessments never quite matched the lectures for me: we had tight deadlines for weekly quizzes and a big practical project that demanded more design thinking than code, and the grading rubric still felt vague, with comments arriving late enough that I’d already moved on to the next topic. Still, the course did fill some gaps I had, especially around getting a realistic sense of what a security review looks like, and the hands-on parts with the small incident-response drill helped me connect the dots in a way the lectures often didn’t. The instructor was approachable, which I appreciated, even if the overall grading felt like a friction point I had to navigate more than learn from, and I ended up barely passing after a rough term.

Cyber Security Basics with Dr. Park was clear at first and paced okay, but as mid-semester hit I started slipping behind since attendance faded while juggling a full-time work schedule. The heavy report load felt fair yet exhausting, and collaboration expectations were unclear, which didn’t help someone new to the discipline. I kept thinking it would click, but the prerequisite feel and the time sink kept dragging me down.

Database Systems was a slog more than I expected, but the pacing finally clicked once I got past that early bug in the query optimizer and stopped fighting the theory gaps I had. Data Structures with Prof. Haddad was the main focus of my term, and I kept up with nearly every lecture, but the deadlines stacked up unexpectedly and feedback felt sparse until it was too late; still, office hours saved me during a tough week.

The Database Systems course in my last semester felt a bit rough around the edges; the grading rubric was vague and the prerequisite feel like more than advertised, but I still kept up with a solid B. The midterm crunch didn’t help, and email support was slow, though feedback came quickly enough to revise.

I signed up for Operating Systems mainly because I’m curious how stuff like schedulers and memory management actually work, but the course structure made it feel like you had to hunt for the signal through a lot of noise. My background is strong in coding but weak on theory, so I mostly used the materials when I hit a roadblock or needed a quick reference rather than following every week’s cadence. The setup for the tooling took more effort than I expected, and I ended up spending extra time just getting the environment to run, which didn’t always pay off in deeper understanding. I relied on forums, office hours, and the TAs to parse deadlines and ambiguous instructions, since the admin side left too much room for interpretation and I didn’t want to chase minor ambiguities. By the end I barely passed after struggling most of the term, and I wouldn’t say the delivery left a lasting impression, but I learned a lot in a messy, hard-won way.

So, I’m in the middle of a heavy semester, trying to fit this ML course into short evening study sessions, and I barely passed after I wrestled with it most of the term. Taking Machine Learning in my first semester while juggling a full-time job felt like a sprint with a few long uphill stretches. Dr. Miller’s lectures were solid when I could attend, but the ramp-up quickly got dense, and the prerequisites started accessible and then slammed into real data work, which made catching up on evenings I had left besides work feel impossible at times. The forums helped, yet the responses sometimes sounded dismissive, like my basic questions were treated as off-topic noise, which wore on me. The assessment pattern leaned on heavy reports and time-consuming writeups, more than I expected, and the grading felt opaque—I’d hand in something I thought was clear and be unsure which parts blew the grade. I used the materials selectively—watching a few lectures here and there when a deadline loomed—so I didn’t build a steady study habit, and I ended up relying on one or two project elements rather than the full set of practicals. Compared to another course from the term, this one demanded more solo slog and had me thinking twice about taking it again, even though some code and a tool I learned did stick with me.

I signed up for Database Systems out of curiosity, and the first week left me a bit tangled because I came in thinking I’d need less boilerplate than usual, but the lectures were clearer than I expected and moved at a decent pace. The summer term pacing felt compressed, and I had enough time to keep up, though motivation drifted in and out; the workload was heavy on reports and the feedback came fast, but sometimes the explanations felt thin. Still, I’d recommend it to others who already know a bit of adjacent material and want a solid, practical push.

Linear Algebra was this oddly uneven ride mid-semester, especially since I started the term hopeful about an advanced elective and ended barely passing after struggling most of the term. The course felt disorganized at times—deadlines shifted, and the weekly problem sets piled up with little clear guidance on what exactly the grader was looking for, which made the grading feel opaque. I liked the instructor when they explained concepts in class, but the feedback often arrived too late to help before the next deadline, and the detailed comments came long after I’d already moved on. I used the materials selectively, mostly revisiting the notes and a couple of practice exams, and I’ll admit the ramp-up felt sharper than I expected for something billed as accessible at first. The project on eigenvectors was the most concrete anchor, though the rest blurred into a mix of quick lectures and email replies that didn’t quite hit the mark.

I took Cyber Security Basics as my main focus, and it felt useful for industry work, but the lectures with Dr. Singh were often hard to follow and the feedback came late. The few big assessments mattered a lot, and I cooler managed it alongside full-time work.
